
<br> 
<center><img src="https://i.imgur.com/AbCCpQO.png" width="700"></center>


_____

<a id='TOC'></a>

# Spatial data 

_____

1. [Simple Map](#part1)

2. [Choropleths](#part2)

3. [Merging](#part3) 

4. [Creating spatial points](#part4) 

5. [Interactive maps](#part5) 



_____

```{r klippy, echo=FALSE, include=TRUE}
klippy::klippy(position = c('top', 'right'))
```


<a id='part1'></a>

# Simple map

A map in our course can be a set of polygons, lines, or points. In general, each one represents a **layer** of spatial information, and several layers together make a complex map. Let's start by loading a map of polygons, where each polygon represents a zip code area:


```{r, eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
rm(list = ls()) # start fresh

library(sf) 
link="https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/raw/main/maps/wazipMap.gpkg"
zipMap=read_sf(link, layer='zips')

# preview the first rows
head(zipMap)
```

## File types

As you can see in the link above, the map is stored in a file called **wazipMap.gpkg**. The extension *.gpkg* is a common format for spatial data. Other well-known formats are **.shp** for *shapefiles* and **.geojson** (or simply **.json**) for *GeoJSON* files.

You can use a URL to read *.gpkg* or *.geojson* files directly into R, but this may not work for *.shp* files. This is because a shapefile is actually a **collection of files** rather than a single file, making it less convenient to read from a URL. If you need to share or access a shapefile remotely, it is best to convert it to *.gpkg* or *.geojson* first.

All of these formats are read using the same function: **`read_sf()`**. Note that the `layer` argument is needed when reading a *.gpkg* file (which can store multiple layers), but it is not required for *.geojson* or *.shp* files.

The object **zipMap** contains the following variables:
```{r}
names(zipMap)
```

Every spatial file must have at least two columns: one serving as an identifier (an "id"), and the mandatory **`geom`** column, which holds the geometry information (polygon, line, or point) that R uses for plotting. This file includes additional columns: the zip code of each area (*ZCTA5CE10*), and population figures for the years 2005 and 2015.

We can visualize the polygons using *ggplot2*:

```{r, warning=FALSE}

library(ggplot2)

base=ggplot(data = zipMap) # define the map
base + geom_sf(fill = 'grey80',# plot the geometry
               color = 'black',
               linewidth = 0.2)   

```

## Map projection

Since a map is a two-dimensional representation of a three-dimensional object, every map uses a **projection** — a mathematical method for translating the curved surface of the Earth onto a flat plane. The projection used depends on the geographic location of the map. The current projection for our WA state map is:



```{r}
st_crs(zipMap)$epsg
```

When the EPSG code is **4326**, the map uses geographic coordinates (longitude and latitude in degrees), which is typically the default when downloading maps from the web. This projection is **not suitable for computing distances or areas** in meaningful units (e.g., meters). For that, we need to re-project the map to a suitable coordinate reference system (CRS). You can search the web or ask an AI tool for the right EPSG code for your study area:




```{r}
# EPSG 26910 - appropriate for Washington State
zipMap_proj <- st_transform(zipMap, crs = 26910)
```

The object **zipMap_proj** is actually a new map, but now rightly projected. You can confirm the change:
```{r}
st_crs(zipMap_proj)$epsg
```

_____


[Go to table of contents.](#TOC)

________________


<a id='part2'></a>

# Choropleths


A choropleth is a colored map that represents the spatial distribution of a variable. Colors assign a visual "ranking" to each polygon relative to the others — in other words, **fill color encodes information**.

Coloring polygons is straightforward, but it can also lead to interpretation problems. For example, let's color the polygons using population data from 2005 using the [viridis colors](https://sjmgarnier.github.io/viridis/reference/scale_viridis.html): 

```{r}
base <- ggplot(data = zipMap)

base + geom_sf(aes(fill = POP2005),   # variable used to color each polygon
               color = NA) +           # no polygon borders
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) # apply viridis color scale
```





The map above plots raw counts — the values as they are, with no transformation. Plotting raw counts generally produces a **biased visualization**: color intensity ends up visually correlated with polygon area size (larger polygons tend to have larger counts simply because they are bigger). You can read [this article](https://www.esri.com/about/newsroom/arcuser/understanding-statistical-data-for-mapping-purposes/) for further details.

Raw counts are not an intrinsic property of a polygon — if you split a polygon in two, the count changes. Therefore, we should apply a basic transformation before mapping.

---


## Densities


Using population *density* instead of raw *counts* removes the visual bias caused by polygon size, since density accounts for the area of each polygon. Let's compute the area of each polygon in km²:



```{r}
library(units)

# We use 'zipMap_proj' (projected CRS) to compute area in meters, then convert to km²
# st_make_valid(): ensure all polygons have valid geometry before computation
# st_area():       compute the area of each polygon
# set_units():     convert to km²
# drop_units():    keep only the numeric value, dropping the unit label

zipMap$area_km2 <- drop_units(
  set_units(st_area(st_make_valid(zipMap_proj)), "km2")
)

# compute population density (people per km²)
zipMap$POP2005_density <- zipMap$POP2005 / zipMap$area_km2
```


Plotting the density:
```{r}

base <- ggplot(data = zipMap)   # rebuild base plot to include the new variable

choroPop <- base + geom_sf(aes(fill = POP2005_density), color = NA)

choroPop + scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1)

```



## Ratios Instead of Differences

Let's now represent *change* in population between 2005 and 2015. A first instinct might be to use a **subtraction**:



```{r}
# change represented as an absolute difference (subtraction)
base + geom_sf(aes(fill = POP2015 - POP2005), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1, option = "B")   # "B" = "inferno" palette

```

However, absolute differences are still affected by the original size of the counts — a gain of 500 people means something very different in a zip code of 1,000 vs. one of 100,000. A **ratio**  is a better choice because it expresses change *relative* to the starting value:


```{r}

# change represented as a ratio

base + geom_sf(aes(fill = POP2015 / POP2005), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1, option = "B") 

```
GIven the value "1" represents NO change, we could try a diverging scale:
```{r}
base + 
  geom_sf(aes(fill = POP2015 / POP2005), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "black",      # Color for values < 1 (Population decrease)
    mid = "white",        # Color for values = 1 (No change)
    high = "#d7191c",     # Color for values > 1 (Population increase)
    midpoint = 1,         # Your specific center point
    name = "Growth Ratio"
  )

## you may try:
  # scale_fill_distiller(
  #   palette = "RdBu",     # Red-Blue diverging palette
  #   limit = c(0.5, 1.5),  # Centering the limits helps keep 1 at the center
  #   direction = 1
  # )
```




_____


[Go to table of contents.](#TOC)

________________


<a id='part3'></a>


# Merging data into a map


## The data


Maps rarely come with rich or interesting data on their own. Most of the time, we need to bring in an external dataset and merge it into the map.


Let's work with data on contributions to candidates and political committees in Washington State. The WA [OpenData portal](https://data.wa.gov/) provides  this dataset on this [page](https://data.wa.gov/Politics/Contributions-to-Candidates-and-Political-Committe/kv7h-kjye).


```{r, eval=TRUE}
linkData='https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/raw/main/data/contriWA.RData'
load(url(linkData))

# NOTE: this is a regular data frame, NOT a map
head(contriWA)
```


Let's check the data types:

```{r, eval=TRUE}
str(contriWA,width = 60, strict.width = 'cut')
```

A few things to address before we can work with this data:

**1. Zip codes are stored as numeric.** We need them as character strings to match the zip code IDs in the map:


```{r}
contriWA$contributor_zip=as.character(contriWA$contributor_zip)
```


**2. The data is in long format by election year.** Let's check which years and parties are available:

```{r}
table(contriWA$election_year,contriWA$party)
```

Let's keep the Democrats and Republicans for 2012 and 2016:

```{r}
contriWA_1216_RD=contriWA[contriWA$election_year%in% c(2012,2016) & contriWA$party%in% c('DEMOCRAT','REPUBLICAN'),]
```

**3. Unused factor levels remain after subsetting.** Let's verify this and then drop them:

```{r}
# unused levels are still present after subsetting
table(contriWA_1216_RD$election_year,contriWA_1216_RD$party)
```

Here we  drop those unused levels:
```{r}
# this is a very important piece of code!!
contriWA_1216_RD$party=droplevels(contriWA_1216_RD$party)
```

## Aggregating Before Merging

To merge this data into a map of zip codes, each row in our dataset must represent **one zip code** — not one individual contribution. We therefore need to **aggregate** the data by zip code, election year, and party:


```{r, eval=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

library(dplyr)

# aggregate by zip code, election year, and party
# counts:        number of contributions
# amountPerCap:  mean contribution amount

contribsWA_agg <- contriWA_1216_RD %>%
  group_by(contributor_zip, election_year, party) %>%
  summarise(
    counts       = length(amount),
    amountPerCap = mean(amount),
    .groups      = "drop"
  )
# then
head(contribsWA_agg)
```


Now let's use `counts` to compute the **percentage** of contributions going to each party per zip code and year. Once computed, `counts` is no longer needed:


```{r}
contribsWA_agg <- contribsWA_agg %>%
  group_by(contributor_zip, election_year) %>%
  mutate(percentage = counts / sum(counts) * 100) %>%
  ungroup()

# remove the raw counts column
contribsWA_agg$counts <- NULL

```

## Checking Coverage Before Merging

Some zip codes in the contributions data may not exist in the map, and vice versa. Let's quantify this mismatch:


```{r}
# not in zipMap 
length(setdiff(contribsWA_agg$contributor_zip,zipMap$ZCTA5CE10))
```
```{r}
# not in contribsWA_agg_W 
length(setdiff(zipMap$ZCTA5CE10,contribsWA_agg$contributor_zip))
```
Only ZIP codes present in both datasets will be retained after merging; note that several locations are lost in this process.


---

## The Merge


```{r, eval=TRUE}

# Always put the MAP on the left side of the merge.
# This ensures the result remains a spatial (sf) object.
# Reversing the order — merge(contribsWA_agg, zipMap, ...) — would lose the geometry.

contrib_zipMap <- merge(zipMap, contribsWA_agg,
                        by.x = 'ZCTA5CE10',
                        by.y = 'contributor_zip')

# now you have
head(contrib_zipMap)
```


The result, **`contrib_zipMap`**, is a new spatial object that combines the zip code geometries with the aggregated contribution data, ready for choropleth mapping.


Let's explore the contributions percapita:

```{r, eval=TRUE}

base <- ggplot() + theme_void()

base + geom_sf(data = contrib_zipMap,
               aes(fill = amountPerCap)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +       
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) +
  labs(fill = 'Contribution Per Capita')


```


Notice the map gives no visual reference for areas that were lost during the merge. A better approach is to add a **background layer** showing the full state boundary, so that unmatched zip codes appear as white gaps:


```{r, message=FALSE}

# load a simple WA state boundary as a background layer
wa_simple <- read_sf('https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/raw/refs/heads/main/maps/WA_State_Boundary.geojson')

# use wa_simple as the base layer (white fill = no data)
base <- ggplot(wa_simple) +
  geom_sf(fill = 'grey') +
  theme_void()

base + geom_sf(data = contrib_zipMap,
               aes(fill = amountPerCap),
               color = NA) +               # no polygon borders
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) +
  labs(fill = 'Contribution Per Capita\n(grey: no information)')

```

Now let's explore **party involvement** using the percentage of contributions going to each party (with some legend customization):


```{r, eval=TRUE}

base + geom_sf(data = contrib_zipMap,
               aes(fill = percentage),
               color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) +
  labs(fill = 'Involvement (%)\n(grey: no information)') +
  theme(legend.title    = element_text(size = 8, hjust = 0.5),
        legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.key.width = unit(1, "cm")) +
  guides(fill = guide_colourbar(title.position = "top"))
```

With a continuous color scale it is still difficult to draw clear conclusions from the map. **Discretizing** the variable into categories can make patterns easier to read and communicate.


# Discretizing

Let's organize `amountPerCap` into four categories. We follow these steps:


1. Explore the variable to find cutpoints. 

We use `fivenum` (minimum, lower hinge, median, upper hinge, maximum) to summarize the distribution by group:


```{r}
do.call(data.frame,
        aggregate(data=contrib_zipMap,
                  amountPerCap~election_year +party,
                  fivenum))
```

The distribution is heavily right-skewed, so **powers of 10** are a natural choice for cut points. Let's confirm visually:

```{r}
ggplot(contrib_zipMap) +
  aes(x = amountPerCap) +
  geom_density() +
  facet_wrap(~ election_year + party, ncol = 1) +
  scale_x_log10()
```



2. Cut the variable:

Based on that information, let me customize the cuts:
```{r}
# vector of data breaks
customCuts=c(0,10,100,1000,10000)
```

Now create the discrete version:
```{r}
customLabels <- c("Up to $10", "$10–$100", "$100–$1,000", "Over $1,000")

# create the discrete variable directly with labels inside cut()
contrib_zipMap$amount_perCap_cat <- cut(contrib_zipMap$amountPerCap,
                                        breaks        = customCuts,
                                        labels        = customLabels,
                                        include.lowest = FALSE)
#
#
# see the distribution
table(contrib_zipMap$amount_perCap_cat,
      contrib_zipMap$election_year,
      contrib_zipMap$party)
```


Each category has at least one observation. The extreme categories (below $10 and above $1,000) are rare, while the middle ranges are the most common.


3. Plot with a sequential palette

Choose a sequential palette from [here](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/scale_brewer.html):


```{r}

base + geom_sf(data = contrib_zipMap,
               aes(fill = amount_perCap_cat),
               color = NA) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "YlOrRd") +
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) +
  labs(fill     = "US$ Per Capita\n(grey: no information)",
       title    = "Contributions to Republicans and Democrats: Not much difference",
       subtitle = "WA State zip codes — contributions in 2012 & 2016") +
    theme(legend.position = "left")

```



How to highlight the least common?

Using the same variables in different dimensions may help:

```{r}

base <- ggplot(wa_simple) +
  geom_sf(fill = 'white') +
  theme_void()

base + geom_sf(data = contrib_zipMap,
               aes(fill = party),
               color = NA) +
  facet_grid(election_year ~amount_perCap_cat ) +
  labs(fill     = "Party receiving contribution",
       title    = "Contribution amounts",
       subtitle = "WA State zip codes — contributions in 2012 & 2016") +
  theme(legend.position = "left")
```

What about annotating? Let's use *geom_sf_text* to add zip code labels only for the two extreme categories to avoid visual clutter:

```{r}
ZipOk=c("Up to $10",  "Over $1,000")
contrib_zipMap_SUB=contrib_zipMap[contrib_zipMap$amount_perCap_cat%in%ZipOk,]
```



```{r, warning=FALSE}
#

base + geom_sf(data=contrib_zipMap,
               aes(fill=amount_perCap_cat),color=NA) + 
               labs(fill="US$ PerCapita \nAreas with NO Info in white",
                    title='Top contributions increasing for democrats in 2016',
                    subtitle='WA State counties, contribution in 2012 & 2016',
                    caption="Source: WA State Open Data (https://data.wa.gov/)")+
                    scale_fill_manual(values = customCols) +
                        facet_grid(party~election_year) +
    theme(legend.position = "left") +
    # annotating
    geom_sf_text(data=contrib_zipMap_SUB,
                 aes(label=ZCTA5CE10,
                     color=amount_perCap_cat),
                 check_overlap = T,
                 size=1.5,
                 nudge_y = 0.15) +
            scale_colour_manual(values=c("black", "red")) + 
            guides(color=F)
```

As this section shows, making a map is technically straightforward — but finding a clear, honest story to tell with it takes time and iteration.






[Go to table of contents.](#TOC)

________________


<a id='part4'></a>

# Plotting coordinates

The dataframe **contriWA_1216_RD** contains columns with geographic coordinates (longitude and latitude). Let's convert it into a **spatial points dataframe**, making sure it uses the same coordinate reference system (CRS) as our zip code map:

```{r, eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
mapCRS <- st_crs(zipMap)   # extract the CRS from our zip code map, use it below

contriWA_1216_RD_points <- st_as_sf(contriWA_1216_RD,         # source data frame
                                     coords = c("Lon", "Lat"), # columns with coordinates
                                     remove = TRUE,            # drop the original Lon/Lat columns
                                     crs    = mapCRS)          # assign the same CRS as the map
```

The new spatial object has the same columns as before — except the `Lon` and `Lat` columns have been replaced by a single `geometry` column:

```{r, eval=TRUE}
names(contriWA_1216_RD_points)
```

Unlike a regular dataframe, this spatial object is now an `sf` (simple features) object:

```{r, eval=TRUE}
class(contriWA_1216_RD_points)
```

We can confirm its geographic properties with:

```{r, eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE}
st_crs(contriWA_1216_RD_points)
```


Now let's plot the points on top of the WA state zip code map:

```{r}
base <- ggplot(wa_simple) +
  geom_sf(fill = 'white') +
  theme_void()

contributors <- base +
  geom_sf(data  = contriWA_1216_RD_points) +
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) + labs(title ="Location of contributors")

# see
contributors
```

Individual contributions are plotted as points across WA State. Arguably, the western side of the state has the most contributors — but given that  points could stack on top of each other it is impossible to judge the true local concentration. We can say that both parties draw contributions predominantly from western WA, but beyond that, the overplotting prevents any deeper reading. We cannot tell whether there is one dominant cluster or several, nor can we compare the intensity between parties or years. 

A **kernel density estimate (KDE)** replaces the raw points with a smooth surface that shows *where contributions are most concentrated*. Before computing it, we need to extract plain `X` and `Y` coordinate columns from the `geometry` column, since `stat_density_2d()` cannot read directly from an `sf` geometry:


```{r}
# extract X (lon) and Y (lat) from the geometry column into plain columns
contriWA_1216_RD_points <- cbind(
  contriWA_1216_RD_points,
  st_coordinates(contriWA_1216_RD_points)   # adds columns named X and Y
)
# you get:

head(contriWA_1216_RD_points)
```

Now, time to plot:

```{r}

base +
  stat_density_2d(
    data  = contriWA_1216_RD_points,
    aes(x = X, y = Y,
        fill  = after_stat(level)),
    geom  = "polygon"
  ) +
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) +
  labs(title    = "Contributions concentrated in the west")
```
```{r, eval=FALSE}
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option    = "B",
                       direction = -1,
                       name      = "Density")
```




By replacing raw points with a kernel density estimate, we smooth the overplotting into a surface that shows where contributions are truly concentrated. Now the picture is clear: there is one dominant hot spot for both parties — the Seattle/Puget Sound area. Democrats show a tighter, more intense core; Republicans show a slightly broader spread. However, because the density scale is absolute (shared across all panels), the dominant western cluster completely overwhelms any other signal. If there are secondary clusters elsewhere in the state — say, in eastern WA — they are invisible here. We need to look more carefully.

By **normalizing the density within each panel** (scaling independently from 0 to 1), we allow weaker secondary patterns to emerge that were previously drowned out:



```{r, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
base +
  stat_density_2d(
    data     = contriWA_1216_RD_points,
    aes(x    = X, y = Y,
        fill = after_stat(ndensity)),
    geom     = "raster",
    contour  = FALSE,
    alpha    = 0.7,
    h        = c(0.8, 0.8)
  ) +
  # overlay the state boundary ON TOP of the raster to restore geographic context
  geom_sf(data = wa_simple, fill = NA, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option    = "B",
                       direction = -1,
                       name      = "Relative\nDensity") +
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) +
  labs(title    = "Contributions are overwhelmingly concentrated in western WA — for both parties",
       subtitle = "Normalized kernel density — WA State, 2012 & 2016",
       caption  = "Source: WA State Open Data (https://data.wa.gov/)")
```


Now a new detail becomes visible: **Republicans have a secondary cluster in eastern WA** (~118°W, likely the Spokane area) that was completely hidden in the previous map. Democrats, by contrast, remain almost exclusively concentrated in the west. This west/east partisan divide is subtle — eastern WA contributions are far fewer in absolute terms — but it is real, and it would have gone unnoticed without normalization.

> ⚠️ **Important caveat:** because each panel is now on its own scale, you can no longer compare magnitude *across* panels. This map answers *"where within each group?"* — not *"which group contributes more?"*. The absolute and normalized maps are therefore **complementary, not interchangeable**.

---

The progression from Map 1 to Map 3 illustrates a core principle of spatial visualization: **each design choice reveals something and hides something else**. Raw point maps show the data honestly but collapse under overplotting. Absolute density surfaces expose the dominant pattern but suppress secondary structure. Normalized density surfaces uncover hidden clusters but sacrifice cross-group comparability. Used together, the three maps converge on a single conclusion: **political contributions in WA State are a western, urban phenomenon for both parties** — but Republicans have a foothold in eastern WA that Democrats largely do not.

---

_____


[Go to table of contents.](#TOC)

________________


<a id='part5'></a>


# Interactive maps

Interactive maps are useful when you need zooming in. I will use **leaflet** library:

```{r, eval=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(leaflet)

# theMAP subset: republicans 2012
map_Rep2012=contrib_zipMap[contrib_zipMap$election_year==2012 &
                           contrib_zipMap$party=='REPUBLICAN',]

# create palette to fill polygons
paletteFun=colorFactor(palette = "YlOrRd", 
                       domain = map_Rep2012$amount_perCap_catLabel)

#popup labels when using your cursor
popUp_labels <- sprintf("<strong>%s</strong>",
                        map_Rep2012$ZCTA5CE10) %>% lapply(htmltools::HTML)

# the base map: the WA boundaries (optional)
base_map = leaflet() %>% 
            addTiles()%>%
             addPolygons(data = wa_simple,
                         fillColor = "white",
                         weight = 0,
                         fillOpacity = 1)

# adding a layer (main map layer)
Rep2012 = base_map %>%
         addPolygons(data=map_Rep2012,
                     stroke = F, # borders of polygon?
                     opacity =  1, # # the closer to 0 the more transparent
                     fillOpacity = 0.8, # color brigthness
                     fillColor = ~paletteFun(amount_perCap_catLabel),# coloring
                     label = popUp_labels, 
                     labelOptions = labelOptions(
                         style = list("font-weight" = "normal"),
                         textsize = "15px",
                         direction = "auto"))
                    

Rep2012
```

You may need a legend:
```{r, eval=TRUE}
Rep2012 %>% addLegend(data=map_Rep2012,
                        position = "bottomright",
                        pal = paletteFun,
                        values = ~amount_perCap_catLabel,
                        title = "Contributions<br>(No contributions in white)",
                        opacity = 1) 

```

Let's create some kind of interactive legend:

* Create subsets: here for democrats in 2012

```{r}
# filtering the zip map
contribWA2012=contrib_zipMap[contrib_zipMap$election_year==2012,]
contribWA2012_demo=contribWA2012[contribWA2012$party=='DEMOCRAT',]

# these the subsets of interest
# remember we have: 'theLabelsForLevels' 
map10=contribWA2012_demo[contribWA2012_demo$amount_perCap_catLabel==theLabelsForLevels[1],]
map100=contribWA2012_demo[contribWA2012_demo$amount_perCap_catLabel==theLabelsForLevels[2],]
map1000=contribWA2012_demo[contribWA2012_demo$amount_perCap_catLabel==theLabelsForLevels[3],]
map10000=contribWA2012_demo[contribWA2012_demo$amount_perCap_catLabel==theLabelsForLevels[4],]

```


* Create a layer for each submap, and use the **group** argument. Let's use a color safe [palette] (https://colorbrewer2.org/). Here I will use the colors of the diverging palette "PuOr" for 4 levels (I will copy and paste them into a vector). Notice I will use _transparency_('fillOpacity') for the most common levels:

```{r, eval=TRUE}
# from the brewer website, copy and paste:
PuOr=c('#e66101','#fdb863','#b2abd2','#5e3c99')

# one layer per group

layer1= leaflet() %>% 
            addTiles() %>%
        addPolygons(data=map10,
                    color=PuOr[1],
                    fillOpacity = 1, # no transparency
                    stroke = F,
                    group = theLabelsForLevels[1]) # LAYER as GROUP

layer1_2= layer1%>%addPolygons(data=map100,
                               color=PuOr[2],
                               fillOpacity = 0.5, # transparency!!!
                               stroke = F,
                               group = theLabelsForLevels[2])


layer1_2_3= layer1_2%>%addPolygons(data=map1000,
                                   color=PuOr[3],fillOpacity = 0.5,stroke = F,
                                   group = theLabelsForLevels[3])

layer1_2_3_4= layer1_2_3%>%addPolygons(data=map10000,
                                   color=PuOr[4],fillOpacity = 1,stroke = F,
                                   group = theLabelsForLevels[4])

layer1_2_3_4
```

* Let's add a _button_ that helps rezooming:

```{r, eval=TRUE}
textFun="function(btn, map){map.setView([47.751076, -120.740135], 7)}"

finalZoom= layer1_2_3_4 %>%
    addEasyButton(
        easyButton(icon="fa-home", # a symbol
                   title="Zoom to Level 1",
                   onClick=JS(textFun)))

finalZoom
```

* Add an 'interactive legend' using the groups we previously defined:

```{r, eval=TRUE}

finalZoomLayers=finalZoom %>% addLayersControl(
        overlayGroups = theLabelsForLevels,
        options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE))
finalZoomLayers
```

## Facetting

Facetting is not straightforward. If we use the previous map, we can build another one for democrats:

```{r, eval=TRUE}
# creating sub maps
contribWA2016=contrib_zipMap[contrib_zipMap$election_year==2016,]
contribWA2016_demo=contribWA2016[contribWA2016$party=='DEMOCRAT',]

map10b=contribWA2016_demo[contribWA2016_demo$amount_perCap_catLabel==theLabelsForLevels[1],]
map100b=contribWA2016_demo[contribWA2016_demo$amount_perCap_catLabel==theLabelsForLevels[2],]
map1000b=contribWA2016_demo[contribWA2016_demo$amount_perCap_catLabel==theLabelsForLevels[3],]
map10000b=contribWA2016_demo[contribWA2016_demo$amount_perCap_catLabel==theLabelsForLevels[4],]

# plotting

layer1b= leaflet() %>% 
            addTiles() %>%
        addPolygons(data=map10b,
                    color=PuOr[1],fillOpacity = 1,stroke = F,
                    group = theLabelsForLevels[1]) # LAYER as GROUP

layer1_2b= layer1%>%addPolygons(data=map100b,
                               color=PuOr[2],fillOpacity = 0.5,stroke = F,
                               group = theLabelsForLevels[2])


layer1_2_3b= layer1_2%>%addPolygons(data=map1000b,
                                   color=PuOr[3],fillOpacity = 0.5,stroke = F,
                                   group = theLabelsForLevels[3])

layer1_2_3_4b= layer1_2_3%>%addPolygons(data=map10000b,
                                   color=PuOr[4],fillOpacity = 1,stroke = F,
                                   group = theLabelsForLevels[4])

textFun="function(btn, map){map.setView([47.751076, -120.740135], 7)}"

# Let's not request the HOME button..

finalLayersb=layer1_2_3_4b %>% addLayersControl(
        overlayGroups = theLabelsForLevels,
        options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE))
```

Does this help? You need *leafsync* library:

```{r}
library(leafsync)
finalFacet=sync(finalZoomLayers,finalLayersb,ncol = 2)
finalFacet
```
Let's stop here!


<!-- **Leaflet** is a good option too for spatial points: -->

```{r, eval=FALSE,warning=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
leaflet(contriWA_1216_RD_mapPoints) %>% 
    addTiles() %>% 
    addCircleMarkers(clusterOptions = markerClusterOptions())
```

<!-- The previous map aggregated all the data. Let me split by party for 2016: -->

```{r, warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# subsetting
sub2016=contriWA_1216_RD_mapPoints[contriWA_1216_RD_mapPoints$election_year==2016,]

leafDEMO2016=leaflet(sub2016[sub2016$party=='DEMOCRAT',]) %>% 
    addTiles() %>% 
    addCircleMarkers(clusterOptions = markerClusterOptions())
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE,warning=FALSE}
# for rezoom
textFun="function(btn, map){map.setView([47.751076, -120.740135], 7)}"


leafREPU2016=leaflet(sub2016[sub2016$party=='REPUBLICAN',]) %>% 
    addTiles() %>% 
    addCircleMarkers(clusterOptions = markerClusterOptions())%>%
    # adding the button
    addEasyButton(
        easyButton(icon="fa-home", # a symbol
                   title="Zoom to Level 1",
                   onClick=JS(textFun)))

```

<!-- Now we create our interactive 'facets': -->

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
library(leafsync)
final2=sync(leafDEMO2016,leafREPU2016,ncol = 2)
final2
```


_____

<!-- <a id='part6'></a> -->

<!-- ## Deliverable III (option 1) -->

<!-- This link has data from this [website](https://www.ocpf.us/Home/Index), which, similarly to our previous data, campaign and political finances. This data has a subsample for Boston. -->
<!-- ```{r} -->
<!-- linkBoston="https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/raw/refs/heads/main/data/BostonContrib.xlsx" -->

<!-- bostonCont=rio::import(linkBoston) -->

<!-- #see it -->
<!-- head(bostonCont) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- And you have this map of zip codes: -->

<!-- ```{r} -->
<!-- library(sf) -->
<!-- linkZips='https://raw.githubusercontent.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/refs/heads/main/data/zip_codes.json' -->
<!-- bostonZips=sf::read_sf(linkZips) -->
<!-- #see it -->
<!-- head(bostonZips) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ```{r} -->
<!-- plot(bostonZips[2]) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- You have this variable: -->
<!-- ```{r} -->
<!-- summary(bostonCont$Amount) -->
<!-- ``` -->


<!-- ```{r} -->
<!-- tapply(bostonCont$Amount,bostonCont$`Tender Type Description`,summary) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- Compare the contributions of **two** tender types (you choose which), by zip zode. The results can be interactive, but it is _NOT_ compulsory. -->

<!-- **NOTE**: You need to aggregate the data from the excel file, and then merged that aggregated data into the map. Then, you can plot the choropleths with the data as it is (countinuous) or discretize it before plotting.  -->



<!-- _____ -->

<!-- [Go to table of contents.](#TOC) -->
